ColorEscapeConstants.brief: A namespace containing ANSI escape sequence constants for styling and coloring terminal output.
ColorEscapeConstants.details: The ColorEscapeConstants group encapsulates a comprehensive set of constexpr string constants representing ANSI escape sequences used to style terminal output. These constants include text styles such as underline, flash, and reverse video, as well as foreground and background colors in both standard and bright variants. Organized systematically, they enable consistent and maintainable application of text formatting, colors, and background colors across the entire codebase. This organization simplifies the process of producing visually distinct and readable command-line interfaces, ensuring that styling is applied uniformly and efficiently wherever needed.
ColorEscapeConstants.notes: Constants are grouped by their function: styles, foreground colors, and background colors, with bright variants distinguished for high-contrast or emphasis. This organization promotes clarity and ease of use when applying terminal styles.
ColorEscapeConstants.rationale: This group exists to provide a centralized, standardized set of ANSI escape sequences that facilitate styled and colored terminal output. By consolidating these codes, it ensures consistency, reduces duplication, and simplifies updates or modifications to styling across the application, supporting better maintainability and readability in terminal-based interfaces.
ColorEscapeConstants.name: ColorEscapeConstants

C_UNDERLINE.brief: Represents the ANSI escape sequence to enable underlining in terminal output.
C_UNDERLINE.details: The `C_UNDERLINE` constant holds the ANSI escape code `\\033[4m`, which activates underlining for subsequent text in terminal displays that support ANSI sequences. It is used throughout the codebase to apply underline formatting dynamically, such as when highlighting or emphasizing specific output segments. Being a compile-time constant, it ensures efficient reuse without incurring runtime overhead. This constant is often appended to strings or combined with other escape sequences to control text styling in terminal-based interfaces.
C_UNDERLINE.notes: Stored as a compile-time constant for performance efficiency; used in conjunction with other ANSI codes to manage complex text styles.
C_UNDERLINE.rationale: This constant exists to provide a standardized, reusable way to apply underline formatting in terminal output, facilitating consistent styling across different parts of the application.
C_UNDERLINE.name: C_UNDERLINE

usages.comm_8cc, String interpret_color_code(Ch: Appended to the code string if VIDEO_FLASH_LINE is active, adding underline formatting.

C_FLASH.brief: A constant that holds the ANSI escape sequence to enable flashing text effects in terminal output.
C_FLASH.details: The `C_FLASH` constant contains the ANSI escape code `\\033[5m`, which activates the flashing text attribute in terminal emulators supporting ANSI sequences. It is used in functions that generate styled text output, allowing text to flash or blink to attract attention or indicate status. This escape sequence is typically combined with other styling codes and is sent to the terminal as part of a string to produce dynamic visual effects. Its application is common in scenarios requiring visual emphasis, such as alerts or notifications, and it is often toggled on and off in conjunction with other formatting codes.
C_FLASH.notes: The escape sequence is stored as a string literal and used directly in output functions. Care should be taken to reset or disable the flashing attribute after use to prevent unintended persistent effects.
C_FLASH.rationale: This constant exists to provide a convenient, readable way to insert the flashing text attribute into terminal output, enabling dynamic and attention-grabbing visual cues in command-line interfaces.
C_FLASH.name: C_FLASH

usages.comm_8cc, String interpret_color_code(Ch: Assigned to the code string to indicate flashing text, and conditionally erased based on VIDEO_FLASH_OFF.

C_REVERSE.brief: A constant that stores the ANSI escape sequence for enabling reverse video mode in terminal output.
C_REVERSE.details: The `C_REVERSE` constant holds the ANSI escape code `"\\033[7m"`, which activates reverse video mode, causing the foreground and background colors to swap in terminal displays. It is primarily used in functions that interpret color or formatting codes, such as when a specific character (e.g., 's') signals that subsequent text should be rendered with reverse video. This constant provides a standardized way to insert the reverse video command into terminal output streams and is often used alongside other ANSI codes to achieve various text formatting effects.
C_REVERSE.notes: This escape sequence is part of a set of ANSI codes used for terminal text formatting. It is stored as a `constexpr` pointer to ensure compile-time initialization and efficiency. When used, it typically precedes the text that should be displayed with reverse video effects.
C_REVERSE.rationale: The constant exists to facilitate consistent and readable insertion of reverse video formatting in terminal output, simplifying the process of applying this effect across different parts of the codebase and ensuring maintainability.
C_REVERSE.name: C_REVERSE

usages.comm_8cc, String interpret_color_code(Ch: Used as a color code for reverse video display when 's' is encountered.

CLEAR.brief: A constant that stores the ANSI escape sequence used to reset terminal text formatting to default.
CLEAR.details: The `CLEAR` constant holds the ANSI escape code `\\033[0m`, which is used to reset all text attributes such as color, boldness, and underlining in terminal output. It is commonly employed in functions like `interpret_color_code` to clear any previously applied formatting before setting new styles, ensuring that subsequent text appears with the intended visual attributes. This constant provides a standardized way to revert terminal styling to its default state, facilitating consistent and predictable text rendering across various parts of the application.
CLEAR.notes: Using `CLEAR` ensures that terminal formatting does not unintentionally persist beyond its intended scope, which is especially important when dynamically changing text styles in terminal-based interfaces.
CLEAR.rationale: The constant exists to provide a convenient and reliable means of resetting terminal formatting, preventing style leakage and maintaining clarity in styled terminal output.
CLEAR.name: CLEAR

usages.comm_8cc, String interpret_color_code(Ch: Used to reset or clear previous formatting before applying new color codes, especially in the 'x' case.

C_BLACK.brief: A constant that holds the ANSI escape code for setting terminal text or background color to black.
C_BLACK.details: The `C_BLACK` constant contains the ANSI escape sequence `\\033[0;30m`, which instructs terminal emulators to change the subsequent text or background color to black. It is primarily used in functions responsible for color formatting, such as `interpret_color_code`, to apply black coloring to characters or strings in terminal output. This constant is initialized with a fixed escape sequence and is referenced throughout the codebase to ensure consistent use of the black color code, facilitating uniform and maintainable color formatting logic.
C_BLACK.notes: The constant is stored as a `constexpr` pointer to a string literal, ensuring compile-time initialization and immutability. Its usage pattern typically involves passing it to output functions or concatenating it with other escape sequences for comprehensive color management.
C_BLACK.rationale: This constant exists to provide a clear, reusable, and standardized way to apply black coloring in terminal outputs, simplifying color management and reducing potential errors from hardcoded escape sequences.
C_BLACK.name: C_BLACK

usages.comm_8cc, String interpret_color_code(Ch: Assigned as a color code for black text or background, depending on context.

C_RED.brief: A constant that stores the ANSI escape sequence for setting terminal text color to red.
C_RED.details: This constant holds the ANSI escape code \u001b[0;31m, which instructs terminal emulators to change the text color to red. It is initialized with this specific escape sequence to ensure consistent application of red coloring across various output functions. The constant is commonly used in functions like interpret_color_code, where it is assigned when the character 'R' is encountered, enabling colored output for enhanced readability or emphasis. By centralizing this escape sequence, the code maintains uniformity and simplifies updates if the color formatting needs to change.
C_RED.notes: The escape sequence is stored as a constant pointer to a string literal, ensuring immutability and efficient reuse across the codebase.
C_RED.rationale: This constant exists to provide a clear, maintainable way to apply red coloring in terminal outputs, supporting visual differentiation and emphasis in the application's user interface.
C_RED.name: C_RED

C_GREEN.brief: A constant that stores the ANSI escape code for setting terminal text color to green.

It is used throughout the codebase wherever green-colored output is desired, such as in status messages, highlights, or color-coded displays. Typically, this constant is concatenated with strings to produce colored text output, and it is employed in functions like `interpret_color_code` to apply green coloring based on specific input characters, ensuring a uniform and maintainable approach to color formatting.
C_GREEN.notes: This constant is stored as a `constexpr` pointer to a string literal, ensuring compile-time initialization and efficient usage. It is part of a set of similar constants representing different colors, facilitating consistent color application across the application.
C_GREEN.rationale: The constant exists to provide a clear, reusable, and maintainable way to insert the green color escape code into terminal output, enabling consistent visual cues and highlighting in the application's user interface.

C_YELLOW.brief: Represents the ANSI escape code for setting terminal text color to yellow.

It is used throughout the codebase in functions that handle colored text output, such as in interpret_color_code where it assigns the yellow color to specific characters or conditions. By centralizing this escape code, it ensures consistent use of the yellow color formatting and simplifies modifications if the escape sequence needs to change. The constant is part of a set of similar color codes that facilitate easy and uniform color management in terminal-based applications.
C_YELLOW.notes: The escape code is stored as a C-style string literal and is intended for use in terminal environments that support ANSI escape sequences. Its usage pattern involves concatenating or inserting this code into output streams to change text color dynamically.
C_YELLOW.rationale: This constant exists to provide a clear, reusable reference for the yellow color escape sequence, promoting consistency and reducing errors in color formatting across the application.
C_YELLOW.name: C_YELLOW

C_CYAN.rationale: The constant exists to provide a clear, reusable reference for the cyan color escape sequence, enabling consistent and maintainable colored output in terminal applications. This constant ensures consistent application of the cyan background color throughout the codebase, facilitating clear and uniform visual formatting in terminal displays.
B_CYAN.notes: The escape code is stored as a `const char*`, making it suitable for repeated use without modification. Its usage pattern involves outputting this sequence before the text to be colored and resetting the color after the text as needed.
B_CYAN.rationale: This constant exists to provide a clear, reusable reference for the cyan background color escape sequence, simplifying the process of applying consistent background coloring in terminal output and improving code readability.
B_CYAN.name: B_CYAN

usages.comm_8cc, String interpret_color_code(Ch: Used as a background color code for cyan, assigned in 't' case.

B_GREY.brief: A constant that stores the ANSI escape sequence for setting the terminal background color to grey.
B_GREY.details: The `B_GREY` constant holds the ANSI escape code `\\033[47m`, which, when printed to a terminal, changes the background color to grey. It is used in various text formatting functions to apply a grey background, often in combination with foreground color codes, to enhance the visual presentation of terminal output. The constant is initialized with a string literal representing the escape sequence and is employed in scenarios such as interpreting color characters within text rendering functions, ensuring consistent application of the grey background across different parts of the application.
B_GREY.notes: This constant is part of a set of ANSI escape code constants used for terminal text styling. Its usage pattern involves concatenating or combining with other escape sequences to produce styled output. Since it is a string literal, it is stored in read-only memory and used directly in output streams.
B_GREY.rationale: The constant exists to provide a clear, reusable reference to the grey background ANSI code, simplifying the process of applying consistent background coloring in terminal output and avoiding hardcoded escape sequences scattered throughout the code.
B_GREY.name: B_GREY

usages.comm_8cc, String interpret_color_code(Ch: Used as a background color code for grey, assigned in 'e' case.
